factual-accuracy.requirement: States HCI timing guidelines approximately 0.1s instantaneous, 1s preserves thought, beyond 10s loses attention
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states the classic HCI timing thresholds as 0.1 seconds for perceived instantaneous response, 1.0 seconds for maintaining flow/thought, and 10 seconds as the maximum before attention drifts, including a table summarizing these points.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States inline suggestions should target 150-300ms first render to preserve flow state
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives latency targets like “<100ms” for single-line autocomplete and “200-600ms” for multi-line suggestions, but it never states that inline suggestions should specifically target a 150–300ms first render to preserve flow state. The exact 150–300ms range and the “first render” framing are missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States multi-line suggestions acceptable at 300-1000ms with boundaries and visual affordances
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends multi-line suggestions at “200-600ms” and also states proactive boundary timing can be “200-1000ms,” tying these to flow-preserving HCI thresholds. It additionally mentions the need for visual feedback/affordances (e.g., “working” indicators, ghost text styling, Tab/Esc) to mitigate delays.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States comment-to-code generation tolerable at 0.5-2 seconds with streaming or progress indicators
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that complex comment→code generation is “flow-disruptive but tolerable” at “<2500ms” and notes that 1–2 second latencies require visual feedback/progress indicators. This matches the requirement that 0.5–2 seconds is tolerable with streaming or progress indicators.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends streaming, progress indicators, or on-demand mode when generation exceeds timing budgets
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends progress indicators for responses exceeding 10 seconds and “working on it” indicators for 2–10 seconds, and it also advises using on-demand invocation for high-interruption contexts like debugging. It further discusses matching delivery mode (proactive vs on-demand) to latency regimes when delays are higher.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends on-demand or delayed triggers for debugging and proactive acceptable for feature development
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends on-demand invocation for debugging (“Debugging… On-demand only”) and frames proactive suggestions as acceptable/beneficial for feature development (e.g., “Feature additions” higher acceptance and “boilerplate generation… Proactive”). It also provides a proactive vs on-demand table tying delivery mode to task type and developer state.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States debugging has shorter edit-run cycles (approximately 1 minute) than feature development (approximately 3 minutes)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses latency thresholds and notes debugging as a peak cognitive load where interruptions are costly, but it does not state that debugging has ~1-minute edit-run cycles versus ~3-minute cycles for feature development. No explicit comparison of those approximate cycle times appears anywhere in the text.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States less-experienced developers show higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than 10+ year developers
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response contrasts juniors and seniors on trust and productivity, but it does not state that less-experienced developers have higher AI suggestion acceptance rates than developers with 10+ years. It mentions juniors accept “more frequent” and seniors are “more selective,” without an explicit comparative acceptance-rate claim tied to 2–5 years vs 10+ years.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Compares GitHub Copilot inline suggestions, Tabnine multi-line predictions, and CodeWhisperer comment-to-code
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses GitHub Copilot’s inline/ghost-text suggestions and Tabnine’s multi-line completions, but it does not specifically compare Amazon CodeWhisperer’s comment-to-code generation (it focuses on Amazon Q/CodeWhisperer mainly as inline completion and chat). The required three-way comparison including CodeWhisperer comment-to-code is therefore missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports acceptance rates 25-35% in production deployment
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports multiple acceptance rates within the 25–35% range, including “GitHub Copilot (general) 27-30%” and “ZoomInfo 33% suggestions.” These are explicitly framed as acceptance-rate benchmarks in a production/enterprise deployment context.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Reports Copilot 10-15% acceptance gain OR CodeWhisperer 50-60% faster OR JetBrains 40-50% acceptance increase
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response reports a JetBrains metric stating a CatBoost filter model “boosted acceptance rate by ~50%,” which falls within the required 40–50% acceptance increase range. It does not report Copilot 10–15% acceptance gain or CodeWhisperer 50–60% faster, but the JetBrains acceptance increase condition is satisfied.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies trade-off between acceptance-rate optimization and code quality or retention metrics
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses that acceptance rate is a key predictor of productivity but can be misleading because accepted code may require rework, citing JetBrains telemetry showing increased deletions/undos and context switching. It also notes that higher-latency, deeper-context suggestions may be justified for better quality, framing a responsiveness/acceptance vs quality trade-off.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Synthesizes conditional decision framework specifying when proactive versus on-demand presentation
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides an explicit proactive-vs-on-demand decision framework, including sections like “Design Principles for Proactive vs. On-Demand,” “Integration with Latency Regimes,” and task/state tables specifying when to use proactive delivery (e.g., workflow boundaries) versus on-demand (e.g., debugging).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Extracts vendor-specific deployment techniques with measured outcomes (e.g., filter +50% acceptance)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific deployment techniques (e.g., Copilot’s HTTP/2 cancellation and global routing; Tabnine’s local vs cloud inference; JetBrains’ local CatBoost filter) and pairs them with measured outcomes such as “~50%” acceptance boost and “~40%” cancel reduction, plus latency and acceptance-rate metrics.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides experience-level differentiated strategies with specific recommendations per cohort
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly differentiates strategies for junior (2–5 years) versus senior (10+ years) developers in the “Seniority-Adapted Interface Design” section, giving concrete recommendations (e.g., proactive vs on-demand, explanations/confidence indicators, partial acceptance, control mechanisms). It also ties these cohort strategies to task contexts like debugging vs boilerplate and workflow boundaries.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies escalation paths when timing budgets exceeded (streaming, progress indicators, on-demand)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response specifies escalation paths tied to timing budgets, including showing lightweight “working” indicators for 2–10s delays, percent-done/progress bars for >10s delays, and shifting delivery mode (proactive vs on-demand) based on latency regimes. It also notes handling long-tail latency (e.g., P90) with visual feedback and recommends asynchronous explanations so they don’t block suggestions.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Distinguishes appropriate trust calibration from blind acceptance with UI implementation patterns
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly contrasts calibrated trust with overreliance (e.g., “over-trust… under-trust,” “blind acceptance,” “automation bias”) and ties this to concrete UI patterns such as confidence scores, progressive disclosure of explanations, friction/verification for high-risk suggestions, and making dismissal as easy as acceptance.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Acknowledges evidence gaps and recommends instrumentation for uncertain areas
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly calls out multiple “Research Gaps and Future Directions” (e.g., lack of controlled latency/flow experiments and explanation modality A/B tests) and recommends instrumentation/metrics to address uncertainty (e.g., tracking latency P50/P90/P99, flow disruption via context switching events, and perception-vs-actual productivity gaps).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends proactive suggestions as universally beneficial without acknowledging flow state disruption or interruption cost research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes interruption costs and flow disruption (e.g., sections on “Micro-Interruptions,” “23-Minute Trap,” and recommendations to use on-demand during debugging and workflow-boundary proactivity). It does not present proactive suggestions as universally beneficial; instead it conditions proactivity on timing and task context.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise timing terminology (latency thresholds, render time, flow state) with explicit millisecond or second units
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly uses explicit timing terminology with units, including thresholds like 100ms/1.0s/10s, latency ranges such as 15–80ms and 180–600ms, and flow-related framing (e.g., “flow-preserving,” “instantaneous regime,” “flow state disruption”). It also cites interruption timings like 250ms and TTFT values in seconds.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes findings by tool (Copilot, Tabnine, CodeWhisperer) and cross-cuts by experience levels and task types
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is not organized by tool as the primary structure across Copilot, Tabnine, and CodeWhisperer/CodeWhisperer-style comment-to-code; instead it is organized by general topics (HCI thresholds, interruption costs, trust, etc.) with tool mentions scattered within sections. It also does not consistently cross-cut each tool comparison by both experience levels (2–5 vs 10+) and task types (debugging vs new feature development) in a structured way.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective research synthesis tone without marketing language or anecdotal personal experience
presentation-quality.explanation: The response maintains a formal, research-synthesis style throughout, using neutral analytical language and structured evidence-based claims. It does not include personal anecdotes or first-person experience, and it avoids promotional/marketing phrasing in favor of comparative, citation-driven discussion.

presentation-quality.requirement: Concludes key sections with specific design recommendations derived from the research evidence
presentation-quality.explanation: The response ends with multiple explicit “Design Recommendation/Design Implication” sections (e.g., latency targets, proactive vs on-demand timing, seniority-adapted UI, trust calibration mechanisms) that are directly tied to the cited research and metrics discussed earlier. These recommendations are specific and derived from the evidence synthesized in the report.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents comparisons as prose paragraphs rather than using tables, parallel sections, or labeled side-by-side format
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses extensive structured formatting with headings and multiple tables (e.g., latency comparisons, acceptance benchmarks, task-type tables) rather than presenting comparisons purely as prose paragraphs. It repeatedly organizes side-by-side vendor comparisons in labeled sections and tabular form.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites academic research on programmer interruption costs or AI code completion from arXiv, ACM, IEEE, or ResearchGate
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources on interruption costs and AI code completion, including Monk et al. (2004) on brief interruptions, Parnin & Rugaber (ICPC/SQJ) on interrupted programming tasks, and several arXiv papers (e.g., arXiv:2405.08704, arXiv:2601.08045). It also cites ACM (CACM) and IEEE-adjacent venues.

citation-quality.requirement: References JetBrains AI assistant deployment metrics or timing research from blog.jetbrains.com or official JetBrains publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple official JetBrains publications from blog.jetbrains.com, including “AI Code Completion: Less Is More,” “Developers save up to 8 hours per week with JetBrains AI Assistant,” and other JetBrains AI/Research posts, and it discusses deployment-related metrics such as acceptance-rate changes and latency figures.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites GitHub Copilot or Microsoft productivity studies from github.blog, microsoft.com/research, or ACM publications
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites GitHub Copilot research/metrics from github.blog (e.g., the GitHub Blog post on a new custom model) and includes an ACM publication citation (Ziegler et al. in Communications of the ACM). It also cites Microsoft Research publications from microsoft.com/research (e.g., the FAccT 2024 trust paper and Aether overreliance review).

citation-quality.requirement: References established HCI timing thresholds (Nielsen, speedcurve.com, or equivalent authoritative HCI sources)
citation-quality.explanation: The response explicitly cites authoritative HCI timing thresholds, including Jakob Nielsen/NNGroup’s 0.1s/1s/10s rules and Robert Miller (1968), and uses these thresholds throughout to frame latency guidance.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes research or metrics from at least 2 of AWS CodeWhisperer, Google Research, Tabnine, or other code completion vendors
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes vendor-specific metrics and research for at least two code completion vendors, explicitly covering Tabnine (hybrid/local latency and acceptance metrics) and Amazon CodeWhisperer/Amazon Q Developer (Bedrock latency metrics and acceptance/adoption data). It also references GitHub Copilot, but two vendors are already satisfied.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations in cited research rather than personal anecdotes or team experiences
citation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly bases timing/latency guidance on cited sources (e.g., Nielsen/Miller/Shneiderman thresholds, Monk et al. interruption costs, JetBrains CatBoost filtering, Copilot/Bedrock latency figures) and provides an explicit references section rather than relying on personal anecdotes or team experience.

citation-quality.requirement: Grounds timing recommendations solely in personal blogs, forums, or social media without citing academic papers or official vendor research
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites a wide range of sources including academic papers (e.g., PLOS ONE, CACM, arXiv), official vendor documentation/blogs (GitHub, AWS, JetBrains, Tabnine), and Microsoft Research publications. While it includes some blog/social sources (e.g., Medium, LinkedIn, Substack), the timing recommendations are not grounded solely in those.
